question: Define the problem of imbalanced data in a classification context and briefly explain two simple sampling techniques (one oversampling and one undersampling) to address it.
expectedPoints: Definition: Imbalanced data refers to a classification problem where the number of observations in one class (the majority class) is significantly higher than in other classes (the minority class).
Undersampling technique: Randomly removing examples from the majority class to reduce its size and balance the class distribution. (Note: Risk of losing potentially important information).
Oversampling technique: Randomly duplicating examples from the minority class to increase its size and balance the class distribution. (Note: Risk of overfitting to the specific examples copied).

question: Explain the fundamental difference between data-level and algorithm-level approaches for handling imbalanced datasets. Provide one specific example for each category and describe a potential drawback of that example.
expectedPoints: Fundamental Difference: Data-level methods (e.g., sampling) alter the training data distribution itself, while algorithm-level methods (e.g., cost-sensitive learning) modify the learning algorithm to be more sensitive to the minority class.

Data-level example: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique). It generates synthetic examples for the minority class instead of just copying. Drawback: Can increase overlap between classes and potentially create noisy samples.

Algorithm-level example: Cost-Sensitive Learning. It assigns a higher misclassification cost to the minority class, penalizing the model more for errors on those points. Drawback: Requires careful tuning of the cost matrix, which can be non-trivial.

question: Why is accuracy often a misleading performance metric for imbalanced classification problems? Name two more appropriate evaluation metrics and explain why they are better suited.
expectedPoints: Why accuracy is misleading: A model that simply predicts the majority class for all examples can achieve a very high accuracy (e.g., 99% for a 1% minority class), making it a poor indicator of performance on the class of interest.

Better Metric 1: Precision and Recall (and F1-Score). These metrics focus on the correctness of the positive (minority) predictions rather than the overall correctness, providing a clearer picture of the model's ability to identify the minority class.

Better Metric 2: Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC). It measures the model's ability to distinguish between classes across all possible classification thresholds, making it robust to imbalance as it is not dependent on a single fixed decision point.
